Fort Macleod holds a founding place in Alberta's story. In 1874 the North-West Mounted Police built their first fort here, on the Oldman River, and the town that grew up around it became a ranching and trading hub for the surrounding grasslands. Today about 3,072 people live here, roughly 164 kilometres south of Calgary at the junction of Highway 2 and the Crowsnest Highway (Highway 3) - a crossroads that still carries travellers toward the Rocky Mountains and the U.S. border.

The town's most striking feature is its downtown. Fort Macleod's Main Street, lined with brick and sandstone buildings dating to the late 1800s and early 1900s, is a designated Provincial Historic Area - the first of its kind in Alberta. The restored Empress Theatre, opened in 1912, still runs as one of the oldest operating theatres in the province. Just 18 kilometres to the northwest sits Head-Smashed-In Buffalo Jump, a UNESCO World Heritage Site that draws visitors from around the world.

That mix of old and new shapes the work {COMPANY_NAME} does here.
